data.title: Lea Salonga on Disney roles, crying at KPop Demon Hunters and discrimination she’s faced
data.description:
“Crying like a baby” is not the kind of reaction in-flight entertainment usually evokes, but when Lea Salonga was watching KPop Demon Hunters – in which she has a minor singing role – on a recent trip, that was what happened.
“I wept,” recalls the 54-year-old Filipino singer and actress, famed for being the singing voice behind two beloved Disney characters: Jasmine in Aladdin (1992) and the eponymous heroine in the 1998 movie Mulan.
“I watched it on a flight, and I was like, ‘Why is this movie making me cry so much?’”
Another work that made her “cry buckets” was Maybe Happy Ending.
“It’s quite the successful South Korean musical, and then it made its way to Broadway and it won all of the things. It’s such a gem.
“If there’s anything that those productions have proven, it’s that there is a demand for this kind of [Asian] storytelling. There is a demand to learn about another culture.”

Salonga will return to Hong Kong for a special one-night-only concert on January 10, 2026, at Hong Kong Disneyland for the park’s anniversary celebrations, two decades after she performed for the park’s grand opening.
She will sing some of her all-time classics, such as “Reflection” from Mulan, whose lead character is still dear to her heart.
By voicing two landmark Disney heroines, she catalysed a new era of diversity and inclusivity for Asian performers in animation.
“[Mulan] is Asian in the way I am Asian, and it changes the image that says a lot of folks have about people like us. You just never really know what kind of joy it’ll bring to another human being.
“It makes me so happy knowing that there’s going to be this five-year-old listening to that music.”
The KPop Demon Hunters soundtrack has earned five Grammy nominations for 2026, while Maybe Happy Ending won six Tony Awards in 2025. It is this global recognition for Asian-led productions that Salonga says fills her with so much hope.

The project that Salonga is most anticipating is her work on the forthcoming DreamWorks Animation film, Forgotten Island. It will be the studio’s first production to centre on Filipino culture, featuring the story of two best friends in the magical world. She has already begun recording for the film, which is scheduled for release in autumn 2026.
No further information has been officially released, and while Salonga remains tight-lipped about her role, she shares that she fell in love with the character immediately.
The production comes 37 years after Salonga first shattered Broadway’s bamboo ceiling with her performance as Kim in Miss Saigon, during a time that held limited opportunities for Asian performers.
Decades on, she has seen a tangible increase in Asian representation on stage and screen, marking a shift from the early days of her career.
Back then, even as the first Asian actress to receive a Tony Award and the first Filipino to have a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, she faced discrimination in the industry because of her racial background.
“I’ve been refused an audition just because of who I am and where I’m from, and that had never happened to me before, and this was already after I had a Tony in my hand,” she says.
“When you walk into an audition room as a person of colour, you’ve got to be twice as good as everybody else in the room, and you’re not entitled to anything. Be ready to be rejected a million times.”
She has given the same advice to her son Nic, who is also pursuing a career in acting and singing.
“I give him the real score,” she says. “It’s like, just because you’re my kid, do not expect anything to happen.”
Offstage, Salonga has been a vocal advocate for authentic representation. She has publicly criticised Hollywood for “whitewashing” and used her platform during the Covid-19 pandemic to speak out against anti-Asian racism and gendered violence.

She has lent her voice to two key initiatives: a 2020 NBC Asian-American town hall condemning xenophobia, and a 2021 forum, “#AAPI Women Strong”, where she joined US politician Hillary Clinton and others to dissect how xenophobic violence disproportionately affects Asian-American and Pacific Islander women.
She notes a significant surge in visibility for Asian performers on Broadway right now, where productions like Here Lies Love, which Salonga starred in, can feature an entirely Asian cast and where Filipino actors hold principal roles.
“I’d like to think that, thanks to these shows and also Hamilton or Les Misérables, which have cast Asians as leads, it’s not that big of a deal now that Asian actors are in this space, as long as race is not that uncredited character.
“If that can happen for one show, it can happen for another show, so I’m hopeful that we’ll get to see more storytelling from that part of the world. That is what I’m looking for: original, Asian stories.”

data.title: Families facing heartbreaking uncertainty in aftermath of Hong Kong fire tragedy
data.description:
Leaving Prince of Wales Hospital in Sha Tin on Thursday, unaware of the whereabouts of some of his relatives, a 76-year-old man surnamed Lam was left uncertain how to arrange the funeral of his older brother, who had just died in Hong Kong’s worst fire in seven decades.
He arrived at the interdepartmental help desk in the hospital lobby early in the morning to look for his sister-in-law and nephew, who had lived with his brother, 80, in the same flat at Wan Cheong House.
“They haven’t picked up the phone since yesterday. I’m so worried that they are still in the house,” Lam said.
Lam was among scores of families anxiously waiting for news about their loved ones or for updates on those being treated for injuries from the massive fire in Wang Fuk Estate in Tai Po, which had continued to burn for a second day.
As of 2.50pm on Thursday, the death toll had risen to 55, with an unknown number of people still trapped inside the seven residential tower blocks that were ablaze. Only one block had been spared from the inferno.
Earlier in the day, hospital officers told Lam that there were no records showing his sister-in-law and nephew had been admitted.
Lam knew he would have to break the bad news to his other siblings that the pair remained unaccounted for as of 10am.
Asked what he could do next, he said: “We don’t know whether we should do [the funeral] for one or for three.”

Lam recalled receiving the news of his brother’s death on Wednesday evening.
“Life happens,” he said.
Holding a tissue in his hand, he said he wanted to know where his brother’s body had been found – whether he was in the house or in the hallway trying to escape.
The help desk had been set up to assist relatives of the injured and deceased.
A Post reporter observed at least 20 members of the public entering the booth throughout the morning, while officers were seen shaking hands with relatives as a gesture of sympathy.
At the emergency department, ambulances carrying rescued residents arrived at about 10.30am, one after another.
Patients brought in from the scene were easily identified by a card attached to their bodies.
What distinguished them from other patients was the colour of their skin, their faces and limbs appearing slightly darker – almost as if coated in grey ash.
Three patients were breathing through oxygen masks and lying on stretchers with their eyes closed.
There was a distinct odour in the air as the ambulance doors opened.
The burnt smell lingered briefly even after the patients were wheeled into the emergency room.

data.title: South Korea sanctions Prince Group’s Chen Zhi over Cambodia scam rings
data.description:
The South Korean government on Thursday imposed sanctions on individuals and companies across Southeast Asia tied to regional crime rings, in response to a surge in crimes targeting Korean nationals.
“It is our first unilateral sanction responding to a transnational crime and the single biggest, which shows the government’s willingness to actively respond to online crimes in Southeast Asia,” it said in a statement.
The sweeping measures target 15 individuals and 132 entities believed to have been directly or indirectly involved in online scams, luring schemes and the confinement of South Korean nationals in Cambodia.
According to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the sanctions include the Cambodia-based Prince Group and its chairman Chen Zhi, who oversaw the operation of large-scale scam compounds such as the Taizi and Mango complexes, where many South Koreans were detained.

Sanctions were also imposed on the head of a scam syndicate active in Cambodia’s Bohai region and a gang member identified as a key suspect in the assault, confinement and death of a South Korean university student in August.
Under the measures, designated individuals and entities will face asset freezes, including virtual assets, and restrictions on financial transactions in South Korea. Individuals will face entry bans.
“We will continue to take a pan-government approach and strengthen international cooperation to combat transnational crime and prevent South Korea from being used as a hub for hiding or laundering illicit funds,” the ministry said in a statement.
The sanctions are part of Seoul’s intensified effort to dismantle Cambodia-based crime rings accused of orchestrating job scams and kidnappings of dozens of South Koreans. This initiative includes an agreement with Cambodia to combat online scams and follows the repatriation of over 60 South Korean nationals.


The Prince Group was blacklisted by the United States and the United Kingdom last month, while the mainland China-based Huiyuan Group – also included in South Korea’s sanctions list – was designated a “primary money laundering concern” by the US authorities.
Authorities across Asia, from Singapore to Taiwan and Hong Kong, have also seized assets or detained individuals tied to the group.

data.title: Should Hong Kong abandon bamboo scaffolding after Tai Po fire tragedy?
data.description:
A deadly blaze that ravaged a four-decade-old public housing estate in Tai Po and has killed at least 55 people since Wednesday is once again casting a spotlight on one of Hong Kong’s most iconic yet contentious construction features: bamboo scaffolding.
While the cause of the fire – which consumed seven of Wang Fuk Court’s eight blocks, all of which were undergoing extensive renovation – is still under investigation, focus will be on what caused the ferocious flames to spread so quickly, including the mesh lining that encased the buildings.
According to the Code of Practice for Bamboo Scaffolding Safety, protective nets, screens and plastic sheeting installed on the face of scaffolding should have appropriate fire-retardant properties in compliance with a recognised standard.
In March, the Hong Kong Development Bureau issued a directive requiring that metal scaffolding be used in no less than 50 per cent of all new government public works building contracts.


According to official figures, industrial accidents involving bamboo scaffolds killed 23 people from the beginning of 2018 to January 26, 2025. The Development Bureau additionally said that bamboo scaffolds have intrinsic weaknesses such as “variation in mechanical properties, deterioration over time and high combustibility”, and it encouraged a transition towards metal alternatives.
The Tai Po tragedy has reignited a fresh public debate over the safety of a construction method that is both a proud cultural symbol and a perceived anachronism in a modern metropolis.
Raffaella Endrizzi, an architect and faculty member at the Chinese University of Hong Kong’s School of Architecture, notes that bamboo, as a material, is naturally resistant to ignition, as it retains a significant amount of moisture, which slows combustion.
Under compliant conditions, she explains, bamboo does not behave like the synthetic materials – and other non-compliant elements – that appear to have accelerated the blaze at Wang Fuk Court.
“Bamboo always contains a lot of moisture regardless, and it prevents fire and is harder to burn because it has water, so the bamboo would have to be extremely dry [to ignite],” says Endrizzi, who is also a photographer documenting the city’s bamboo scaffolding culture.
“[The scaffolding] has been up since July last year, so I’m not so sure whether or not it has dried out a lot, but I think the major cause is the net, and not the bamboo.”

Construction industry veterans are pushing back against the government’s increasing preference for metal scaffolding, arguing that the proposed switch is a misguided solution that fails to address the cause of most construction accidents: a lack of quality training, rigorous on-site supervision and strict adherence to safety protocols.
They assert that metal systems introduce their own risks, such as their greater weight requiring cranes and the potential for bolts to loosen if not properly maintained.
The core issue, they argue, is a systemic lapse in safety enforcement and supervision across the industry, a problem that a simple material substitution will not fix.
Timmy So, a long-time member of the Bamboo Scaffolding Association of Hong Kong, expressed the same sentiment, stating that it was actually the scaffolding nets and styrofoam material used in the window sealant, not the bamboo itself, that made the Tai Po fire spread so quickly.
“If we look at the news reports, you will see that even after it was burned, the bamboo there is still yellow, which means that actually the bamboo material never really got that hot.
“Of course, we have concluded for now and will have to wait for someone to check for further details, but it looks like the engineering materials are the problem here.”

For centuries, bamboo scaffolding has been the skeleton upon which Hong Kong is built, scaling the dizzying heights of its skyscrapers and housing estates.
The practice is a craft passed down through generations and its persistent use is not a mere quirk of tradition but a decision grounded in robust practicality, cost-effectiveness, and a unique structural advantage that steel often cannot match.
Bamboo scaffolding also holds an invaluable cultural significance for the city.
Beijing-based Chinese artist Song Dong is in Hong Kong this week for a project about time and perception to mark the 25th anniversary of the Asia Art Archive.
Besides being saddened by news of the fire, he says he also wonders whether this will spell the end of a great Hong Kong tradition.
“We only see metal scaffolding used in [mainland China] and we have always admired how Hong Kong has retained its culture of using bamboo scaffolding. It’s such a great, sustainable material,” he says.
“But our views of the world often change with time and this may be such a time when we think differently about bamboo scaffolding. It’s not just the facts about whether it’s safe or not but also the trauma that it is now associated with.”

data.title: ‘Feeling powerless’: Hong Kong internet users grieve over Tai Po blaze
data.description:
Internet users in Hong Kong poured out condolences for victims and their families affected by the city’s worst fire in decades, expressing shock and disbelief on the second day of the tragedy on Thursday.
Many sent messages of grief and condolences on social media, with several posts offering material support and blood donations.
As of 2.50pm on Thursday, 55 people have died, including a firefighter, as an inferno ripped through all but one of the eight blocks of Wang Fuk Estate in Tai Po that has been burning for 24 hours.
Full-scale rescue work is ongoing, with the elderly and pets continuing to be evacuated from the scorched buildings.
Many online posts praised the bravery of first responders. “I couldn’t help but cry after seeing that even pets were being rescued. Salute to Hong Kong firefighters!” wrote user “lapiswsr” on Threads.
“Every firefighter deserves our utmost respect,” user “cutekatyusha._.030” wrote.
Several viral posts were dedicated to the deceased firefighter Ho Wai-ho, 37, who was one of the first responders to arrive at the scene at 3.01pm on Wednesday.
Ho was found with burns to his face at 4.01pm at Wang Cheong House, the first building to catch on fire, and pronounced dead at 4.45pm on Wednesday.
“Salute to this hero,” user “desertlionk” posted along with a photo of Ho, which received over 19,000 likes on Threads.
Hong Kong celebrities also extended support for the rescue efforts online, with local actress Jessica Hsuan sharing the contact information of nearby community centres hosting displaced residents on her social media accounts.

Meanwhile, mainland Chinese students in Hong Kong expressed their disbelief at the scale of the fire. Zhu Jiale, a master’s student at the University of Hong Kong, said she could not believe the extent of the damage when she passed by Tai Po estate on Wednesday afternoon.
“My heart was gripped; I just wish none of this had happened,” she said.
Such feelings of disbelief extended to residents in neighbouring mainland cities. “It’s suffocatingly painful to see the news,” wrote Chelsea Wang, a resident in Shantou, Guangdong province, on WeChat.
The incident has taken a toll on some Hong Kong residents’ mental health, according to several widely shared online posts. “Feeling powerless and not well. Putting down the phone temporarily to catch my breath again,” Threads user jojo_wu wrote in a post with almost 6,000 likes.
Some internet users shared tips about how to take care of one’s mental health during this period, including for children.
In an Instagram post, the Hong Kong branch of international charity Save the Children advised parents to schedule breaks for their children from watching the constant news about the fire and to encourage them to speak openly about their emotions.
“If your child is already showing signs of distress, switch off the live news for now to prevent them from being overwhelmed by frightening images,” they wrote.

data.title: Bitcoin’s bruising month shows the perils of going mainstream
data.description:
This has been a brutal month for bitcoin enthusiasts. Since hitting an all-time high of US$124,752 on October 7, the original cryptocurrency has plunged 28 per cent and is on track for its worst month since June 2022, when a string of corporate collapses rocked the cryptocurrency industry.
The dramatic sell-off has helped wipe almost US$1.2 trillion off the market capitalisation of the cryptocurrency market, of which bitcoin accounts for 58 per cent. The speed and scale of the decline are matched only by the acuteness of the uncertainty regarding the precise cause of the sell-off.
In a report on November 24, Deutsche Bank provided as many as five plausible explanations. Three stem from bitcoin’s growing institutional acceptance in recent years, which has deepened the digital coin’s links with mainstream finance. “Since October, bitcoin has behaved more like a high-growth tech stock than an uncorrelated store of value,” Deutsche Bank said.
Bitcoin was never an effective hedge against sharp falls in conventional financial assets. Even so, its proponents have long championed its scarcity – bitcoin’s code guarantees the number of coins in existence can never surpass 21 million – as a reason to hold the token at a time when leading central banks are debasing the value of traditional currencies by printing money in perpetuity.
In fact, the early backers of bitcoin, many of them anti-government libertarians, saw decentralised, blockchain-based finance as a democratising force that would broaden financial access and reduce the concentration of economic power and ownership.
However, cryptocurrency’s promise of democratising finance rang hollow. The combination of minimal real-world applications, extreme price swings and the reputational damage stemming from digital currencies’ vulnerability to criminal activity undermined the confidence of even the most diehard fans.


The main reason this decentralisation proved illusory is that bitcoin, as part of efforts to gain respectability and legitimacy, needed to become more integrated into mainstream finance. The financialisation and institutionalisation of cryptocurrency have gained considerable momentum in the past few years.
In January 2024, the launch of exchange traded funds (ETFs) that invest in bitcoin made it easier for investors – both retail and institutional – to access the token, boosting liquidity and reshaping how capital moves into, and out of, bitcoin. Before the start of the sell-off last month, net inflows into the 12 US-listed spot bitcoin ETFs had surpassed US$25 billion this year, increasing the funds’ assets to US$169 billion, according to data from Bloomberg.
Moreover, bitcoin has benefited from a more permissive regulatory environment. Having held digital assets in contempt during his first presidential term, US President Donald Trump has changed his tune, appointing cryptocurrency supporters to key positions and pledging to make the United States the bitcoin superpower of the world.
The Trump family, which has built a rapidly expanding cryptocurrency empire, has profited handsomely from industry-friendly legislation, which includes a legal framework for stablecoins and the designation of certain blockchain-based assets as “digital commodities”. This has made it easier for financial institutions to invest in cryptocurrency.


The reduction in regulatory uncertainty contributed to the faster pace of institutional adoption of bitcoin. JPMorgan noted that “retail investor participation has fallen, and the [sector] now depends more on institutional investors to stabilise flows, reduce volatility and anchor long-term prices”.
Wider institutional acceptance, however, deepened bitcoin’s links with mainstream finance. This increased its correlation with so-called risk assets, turning it into a gauge of sentiment in broader markets. Once seen as a way for investors to diversify their portfolios, bitcoin was swept up in the artificial-intelligence-induced sell-off in equity markets.
Bitcoin ETFs are on track for their worst month of outflows since the funds were launched, with US$3.5 billion in net outflows so far this month. In fact, bitcoin has become sufficiently embedded in mainstream finance that some regulators are worried that a sharper and sustained cryptocurrency sell-off could spark panic and financial contagion.
In a report last month, the European Systemic Risk Board said “growing interlinkages between traditional finance and crypto, coupled with high market concentration in core crypto services, increase the contagion risk for traditional finance”. While bitcoin is not big enough to pose a systemic threat, its sheer staying power while enduring several major market crashes shows it has the potential to inflict damage on mainstream markets.


However, the real problem facing bitcoin right now is that it is neither a fully fledged mainstream asset owned by fund managers nor a purely speculative one propelled by diehard cryptocurrency fans. It is somewhere in the middle and faces the worst of both worlds.
Bitcoin is more vulnerable to sudden shifts in market sentiment as a result of its deeper integration into conventional finance. It also remains a highly volatile asset in a sector where regulation and liquidity are patchy, and fraud and money laundering – not to mention glaring conflicts of interest in the US – still pose significant risks.
Bitcoin is in a vulnerable spot halfway between pure speculation and institutional adoption. Optimists take comfort from how far the cryptocurrency has come despite the succession of dramatic sell-offs. Broader institutional acceptance, moreover, has turbocharged bitcoin’s growth.
Yet if the price of institutionalisation is greater susceptibility to threats facing traditional assets, especially AI-driven tech stocks, bitcoin’s bid to go mainstream is bound to suffer more setbacks.

data.title: As floods ease in Thailand for first time in 1 week, Malaysia braces for new deluge
data.description:
Floodwaters began to recede on Thursday in parts of southern Thailand including Hat Yai, a major city entirely submerged for five days, revealing grim finds of bodies and unprecedented damage to homes and farmland.
The overnight death toll of 33 appears certain to rise from a week of flooding that has hit well over 2.5 million people across the entire southernmost provinces of the country.
Images shared on social media by stranded residents in Hat Yai alone, showing bodies tied to lamp posts to prevent them from being swept away by the raging torrent, suggest the number of dead is likely to rise.
Rescuers called in from across the country have reported finding bodies inside homes that have been inundated with water as high as two to three metres (6.5 to 10 feet).

As swathes of the south remain under water, there are fears that emergency responders have yet to reach communities outside urban areas, across the provinces of Songkhla, Pattani, Yala and Narathiwat.
Experts say the climate crisis is causing heavier, less predictable storms and rain patterns at unusual times of the year, overwhelming flood defences and bringing more frequent misery to vulnerable communities.
Jehabdulloh Jehsorhoh, an artist in Pattani province, shared photos of his flooded gallery on social media, with neck-high waters swamping his property for a second straight year.
“The floods will probably be like this every year,” he told This Week in Asia. “Moving isn’t an option because everywhere gets flooded. I’ll have to adjust how I use the space, but I’ll need to find funding for renovations.”
Local authorities in Hat Yai are feeling the pressure, having been accused of slow responses after record rains – the heaviest single-day downpour for 300 years – dumped down on the city on Friday.


Since Wednesday, Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul has been stationed in the south after accusations that his government had been slow to realise the extent of the crisis.
He is set to call an election early next year and his political rivals have been quick to accuse the government of a fumbling response after hundreds of thousands of people were left stranded on rooftops, without food or water, as rescuers on jet skis and flat-bottomed boats circled in powerful floodwaters, unable to help.
“We can be certain there are many more areas where help has not yet reached, so we still don’t know the full number of casualties,” said Chaturon Chaisang, a veteran Pheu Thai politician and former minister.
A dedicated and experienced incident commander must be appointed to coordinate responses to disasters of this scale, instead of local bureaucrats or government ministers, he added. “Peoples’ lives must come before state delays.”
Southern Thailand is a major rubber producing region and prices have edged up over fears the crop may be devastated by the floods. Tourism has also been hit hard just as peak visitor season is set to start.

Malaysia’s Foreign Minister Mohamad Hasan said on Thursday that 500 more Malaysians are still in southern Thailand despite a major evacuation effort. These include a group of lecturers and students from Universiti Utara Malaysia who have been stranded in their Hat Yai hotel for nearly a week now.
In a video that went viral on Wednesday, several students plead for help as one of them is suffering from thalassaemia and has run out of life-saving medicine.
“Today the currents have slowed and I was informed that several boats will head there to evacuate them,” Mohamad said.
Malaysia on high alert
Floods are also hitting Malaysia’s north, with nearly 30,000 people in seven states evacuated to temporary shelters by Thursday morning.
Kelantan remains the worst-hit. Evacuees are concentrated in the districts of Tumpat, Kota Bharu and Bachok – all located in the flood-prone zone in the eastern stretch of the Malaysia-Thai border.
So far, two have been confirmed dead in the Malaysian floods. The body of a 70-year-old woman was found about 120 metres (400 feet) away after her car was swept away by floodwaters in Kelantan on Wednesday, according to authorities.
Also on Wednesday, Malaysia’s navy urged seafarers to take extra precautions after 28 people were rescued in two separate incidents caused by adverse weather on the peninsula’s west coast.

The country remains on high alert even as flooding began to moderate in the peninsula, with the meteorological department forecasting a fresh round of heavy rain over the rest of the week due to a rare tropical storm that formed in the Malacca Strait.
Tropical Storm Senyar, which formed off the coast of north Sumatra early this week, has been progressively moving towards the peninsula.
Senyar is expected to make landfall on the west coast of the peninsula on Thursday, potentially affecting the country’s industrial belt of Penang, Selangor and Johor and capital city Kuala Lumpur.
“This situation could potentially cause continuous heavy rain, strong winds and rough seas in peninsula Malaysia,” the department’s director general Mohd Hisham Mohd Anip said in a statement on Thursday.
The last time the peninsula was hit by a tropical storm that formed in the Malacca Strait was in 2017, although the deluge was largely limited to Penang.

data.title: China warns against Japan travel citing ‘significant’ surge in assaults as ties spiral
data.description:
As bilateral relations between Beijing and Tokyo continue to spiral, China’s embassy in Japan has advised citizens to put travel plans on hold “in the near term”, citing a recent spike in unprovoked assaults and discrimination.
In a security warning issued on WeChat, the embassy reported a “significant” surge in requests for help since July. “Recently, several Chinese citizens in Japan have reported that they were verbally abused, assaulted and injured for no reason,” it said.
The advisory came as bilateral relations hit their lowest level in recent years after Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on November 7 that suggested that Tokyo would be justified in mobilising its military for “collective self-defence” along with other nations if there was conflict over Taiwan.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Beijing has reacted strongly, lodging protests repeatedly against what it has called Takaichi’s “extremely erroneous and extremely dangerous” remarks. Takaichi, meanwhile, has not backed down and has refused to retract her remarks.
Beijing has also advised Chinese citizens not to travel or study in Japan, halted seafood imports from the country, and cancelled intergovernmental exchanges and cultural events.


Relations between the two nations have long been troubled over historical and territorial disputes. Beijing has accused Japan’s right-wing political factions of glorifying its wartime history and expanding the country’s military capabilities. Mutual public perceptions have also soured.
“In recent years, Japan’s public security environment has continued to deteriorate,” the embassy said.
It cited data from Japan’s National Police Agency that the number of criminal cases increased from 568,000 in 2021 to 738,000 in 2024 – a rise of 30 per cent. The number of felony cases increased by almost 66 per cent during the same period.
Beyond warning Chinese travellers to stay away and instructing citizens already in the country to exercise caution, the embassy called on Japanese authorities to promptly investigate cases and protect the rights of Chinese nationals.
Information from the Civil Aviation Data Analysis System, a platform focused on China’s civil aviation industry, showed that about 12 per cent of China-Japan flights scheduled between November 24 and January 18, 2026, had been cancelled. On some routes, cancellation soared above 50 per cent.
Mao Ning, a spokeswoman for China’s foreign ministry, said on Wednesday that “crimes against Chinese citizens were frequent in Japan”. She said that last week, “Japanese police had arrested five suspects for allegedly assaulting Chinese citizens”.
It was likely a reference to a July attack in Tokyo on two Taiwanese by five Japanese men.
Last Friday, Japan’s foreign affairs ministry rejected claims by Beijing that public safety was deteriorating in the country, citing data from police reports that showed the number of serious crimes committed in Japan between 2023 and 2025 against Chinese nationals had not surged.
On Monday, Japan issued a safety warning to Japanese citizens in China, advising them to be extra cautious when travelling with children and to “avoid places frequently visited by Japanese people as much as possible”.

data.title: Jakarta’s dog meat lovers chew over how to stomach ban: ‘look for its benefits’
data.description:
Sitting at an empty Jakarta stall, Alfindo Hutagaol gobbled down some rice, green sambal and grilled dog meat – a meal that is now banned in the Indonesian capital.
The Muslim-majority archipelago is one of a handful of countries that still permit the sale of dog and cat meat, but a campaign against the practice has been gaining ground in recent years.
While Muslims do not eat dog meat, it is popular in some of Indonesia’s other communities.
This week, Jakarta’s authorities announced a ban in the capital on trade for the consumption of rabies-transmitting animals, including cats and dogs.
The prohibition, with a six-month grace period before enforcement begins, was hailed by animal rights groups who have long lobbied against the trade.
But for Alfindo, “there should be no such prohibition”, he said before the ban was announced.
“God created it to be eaten. Don’t only look for the negative side, but also look for its benefits too,” the 36-year-old said.
Dog and cat meat are not widely consumed in Indonesia, but in some communities, canine meat is considered a home remedy for dengue fever.
The trade “can’t be eliminated suddenly”, said another consumer, 43-year-old Sunggul Sagala, who described dog meat consumption as a “tradition” for some communities.

The Tuesday announcement by Jakarta’s governor covers trade for consumption of all rabies-transmitting animals, including bats, monkeys and civets.
“This is a real example and real commitment from the Jakarta government as a global city that also advances animal welfare,” said Merry Ferdinandez of the Dog Meat Free Indonesia (DMFI) coalition that lobbied for the ban.
The move does not extend to other regions where consumption remains popular, but might “encourage” similar moves, she said.
A poll commissioned by DMFI in 2021 found that 93 per cent of Indonesians rejected the dog meat trade and wanted it banned.
Yet, a DMFI study the following year estimated 9,500 dogs, mostly wild animals caught on the streets, were brought into Jakarta for consumption that year, mostly from West Java, where rabies is endemic.
Nationwide figures for dog and cat meat consumption are not available.
Jakarta has been officially rabies-free since 2004 – one of 11 Indonesian provinces with that status – and the ban was “one of the efforts” to maintain that, said the capital’s food resiliency, maritime and agriculture agency head Hasudungan Sidabalok.
While it is no longer common to see dog meat sold openly either at markets or restaurants, he said 19 restaurants still serve the dish and at least two slaughterhouses are operating, expressing hope the new regulation would “minimise” such practices.
“It’s not an easy step because it is a habit or perhaps a culture for certain communities, which we hope can change,” he said.
“The act of slaughtering or consuming dog meat … is still risky as it can transmit rabies to the slaughterers or consumers.”


Jakarta’s government will publicise the ban during the six-month grace period, after which violators face sanctions ranging from written warnings to revocation of business licences.
Hasudungan said the government is preparing rules to detail further enforcement and sanctions against violators.
Indonesian laws on food and animal husbandry do not explicitly ban the consumption of canine and feline meat, but a 2018 directive issued by the agriculture ministry stated that dog meat is not categorised as food.
Some regions have already imposed their own bans, leading to arrests of traders.
In the Central Javan city of Semarang, which banned the trade in 2022, authorities last year intercepted a truck carrying more than 200 dogs bound for a slaughterhouse and arrested five people.
In Jakarta, increased oversight by authorities has already effectively forced the trade underground.
Restaurants that once openly advertised dog meat dishes no longer do so, nor will owners speak to outsiders about the trade. Meat is also no longer sold openly in markets.
The taboo around the trade means only trusted customers can buy it and the meat is now more expensive than beef, Sunggul said.
Even before the ban, “the fact is that … purchasing dog meat is like looking for drugs”, he said.
There have been no explicit plans for how to deal with animals that may no longer have buyers. A similar ban on dog meat in South Korea has caused problems for traders left with animals that can no longer be sold and are not easy to rehome.
And Alfindo argues dog meat fans might even be forced to prey on Jakarta’s stray dog population if the ban is enforced.
“The government should rethink the policy,” he said, after finishing his dish.

data.title: China’s industrial profits slump in October as price war deepens
data.description:
China’s industrial profits dropped in October after a strong September, according to official year-on-year data released on Thursday, and analysts are pointing to domestic competition and trade tensions weighing on corporate balance sheets.
Profits fell 5.5 per cent from a year ago, reversing a 21.6 per cent surge in September and a 20.4 per cent jump in August, according to figures from the National Bureau of Statistics.
For the January-October period, profits earned by Chinese companies of a certain scale rose by 1.9 per cent, slower than the 3.2 per cent growth in the first nine months, the bureau said.
Profits among manufacturers grew by 7.7 per cent from January through October, while earnings for electricity, heat, gas and water providers expanded by 9.5 per cent. Meanwhile, mining profits saw a year-on-year decline of 27.8 per cent.
The profit setbacks last month were partly due to a high base effect and rapid growth in financial expenses, the bureau said.
A cyclical pause in Chinese holiday spending spikes combined with an expected slowdown of spending in the US – still a major Chinese export market despite the trade war – could have also chilled profits in October, said Charles Chang, a finance professor at Fudan University in Shanghai.
Chen Zhiwu, chair professor of finance at the University of Hong Kong, said that excess production capacity and cutthroat domestic price competition likely contributed to the fall in profits last month.
Price competition, also referred to domestically as “involution”, has concerned officials for much of the year. President Xi Jinping criticised the trend in July as a lever for “unproductive outcomes”.
On Monday, a price-monitoring department under the National Development and Reform Commission stated at a symposium that “disorderly price competition remains a prominent problem in some industries”, with enterprises not always complying with requirements to regulate it.
The commission said it would work with government departments to address the issue and “contribute to high-quality development”.
This year’s US-China trade war, led by rising tariffs and export controls, took a bite last month, too, Chen said, as did a less “aggressive” use of economic stimulus measures that he said were more visible in the first half of the year.
The statistics bureau called for expanding domestic demand and cultivating “new growth drivers” against the backdrop of a “severe, complex international environment intertwined with domestic cyclical and structural contradictions”.
